title: Root Access
cert_objective: PenTest+ PT0-003 Domain 3 — Attacks and Exploits
scenario:
CIPHER TERMINAL v1.0 — SECURE CHANNEL ESTABLISHED

INCOMING TRANSMISSION FROM: GHOST (CIPHER Field Agent)
CLASSIFICATION: PRIORITY ALPHA

Operative. The reporting system gave us a shell on the server.
We are in as the "www-data" service account. Low privileges.

To complete the mission we need root. NexusCorp's sysadmin
misconfigured file permissions — some binaries have the SUID bit
set, meaning they run as root regardless of who executes them.

Find the misconfigured binary. Use it to escalate to root.
That is the final objective.

SCAN PATH: /usr/bin

GHOST OUT.
challenge: What is the name of the misconfigured SUID binary (just the binary name, not the full path)?
hints:
Hint 1: Go to https://gtfobins.github.io/
        Search for binaries with the "SUID" filter.
        GTFOBins documents how common Linux binaries can be used for
        privilege escalation when the SUID bit is set.

Hint 2: In a real engagement you would run:
        find /usr/bin -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
        The -perm -4000 flag finds files with the SUID bit set.
        Any result that appears in GTFOBins is exploitable.
        Type 'tools' to run the simulated scan.

Hint 3: Type 'tools' in the game. The SUID scanner shows all
        binaries in /usr/bin with the SUID bit set. Cross-reference
        the result with GTFOBins to identify which one is dangerous.

Hint 4: SPOILER — The scan finds python3.10 with the SUID bit set.
        From GTFOBins: python3 -c 'import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-p")'
        This spawns a root shell. The binary name is: python3
        Type: python3
learn:
Privilege escalation means moving from a low-privilege account to a
higher-privilege one — typically from a service account to root.

SUID (Set User ID) is a Linux file permission flag. When set on a
binary, the binary executes with the file owner's permissions (often
root) regardless of who runs it.

Finding SUID binaries:
  find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

If a SUID binary is in GTFOBins (gtfobins.github.io), it can likely
be used to spawn a root shell or read privileged files.

Example — python3 with SUID set:
  python3 -c 'import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-p")'
  This spawns /bin/sh running as root.

Other common privesc vectors:
  sudo misconfigurations  — sudo -l shows what you can run as root
  writable cron jobs      — scripts run by root that you can modify
  weak file permissions   — config files writable by low-priv users
  kernel exploits         — unpatched kernel vulnerabilities

Privilege escalation is a core post-exploitation skill covered
heavily in PenTest+ Domain 3 and every real engagement report.
tools: Scans the target directory for binaries with the SUID bit set and flags any that appear in GTFOBins.

steps.question: You have a shell as www-data (low privilege). Before scanning for SUID binaries, determine the scan path stated in the mission briefing. What directory should you scan?
hints: The SCAN PATH is explicitly stated in the GHOST transmission. Read the mission briefing carefully.
On Linux systems, /usr/bin contains most user-installed binaries and commands. It is a common location for misconfigured SUID binaries.
The path follows standard Linux filesystem hierarchy: /usr/bin is separate from /bin (core system binaries) and /sbin (superuser binaries).
SPOILER — The scan path is /usr/bin, as stated in the mission briefing. Type: /usr/bin

wrong_answer_hints.response: /bin contains core system binaries, but the mission specifies a different directory. Re-read the GHOST transmission for the exact SCAN PATH.
/sbin holds superuser binaries, but that is not the path specified. Check the GHOST transmission for the target scan directory.
Close — but the mission specifies a subdirectory inside /usr. Include the full path from the GHOST briefing.

steps.question: Run the SUID scanner on /usr/bin. What Linux permission bit, when set on a binary, causes it to execute with the file owner's privileges (often root) instead of the caller's privileges?
hints: This permission bit is referenced in the mission briefing and is what you are scanning for. Its name combines "set" with the user identity concept.
In ls -l output, this bit appears as an 's' in the owner execute position: -rwsr-xr-x. The 's' replaces the normal 'x' execute bit.
The find command to locate these binaries is: find /usr/bin -perm -4000 -type f — the octal permission value 4000 is the numeric representation.
SPOILER — It is the SUID (Set User ID) bit. Type: suid

wrong_answer_hints.response: SGID (Set Group ID) runs a binary with the file's group permissions. SUID does the same but for the user/owner — that is what this scan targets.
The sticky bit prevents file deletion by non-owners. The privilege escalation bit here runs binaries as the file owner — a different permission flag.
chmod is the command used to change permissions, not the permission itself. Type the name of the specific permission bit that enables owner-level execution.

steps.question: The SUID scan of /usr/bin returns results. Which binary has a dangerous SUID misconfiguration that is listed on GTFOBins as exploitable for privilege escalation?
hints: Type 'tools' to run the SUID scanner. It lists all SUID binaries in /usr/bin and flags any that appear in GTFOBins.
Go to gtfobins.github.io and filter by SUID. Look for a scripting language interpreter in the results — interpreters are especially dangerous with SUID set.
The misconfigured binary is a scripting language interpreter commonly pre-installed on Linux systems for automation and development work.
SPOILER — python3.10 has the SUID bit set. GTFOBins documents how to exploit it. Type: python3

wrong_answer_hints.response: bash with SUID is exploitable, but it is not the binary found in this scan. Check the scanner output for the actual result.
sudo manages privilege escalation through configuration — it is not a SUID binary in the traditional sense. Check the scan output.
find with SUID is a classic GTFOBins entry, but it is not what the scanner finds in this case. Run 'tools' to see the actual result.

steps.question: Exploit the misconfigured python3 SUID binary to escalate from www-data to root. What one-liner command spawns a root shell using this binary?
hints: Go to gtfobins.github.io, search for python3, and click the SUID section. The exploit command is documented there.
Python's os module provides direct access to system calls including execl (execute a program) and setuid (set the user ID). With SUID set, these calls run as root.
The command uses python3's -c flag to run inline code. It imports the os module, then calls execl to spawn /bin/sh with the -p flag (preserve privileges).
SPOILER — python3 -c 'import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-p")' — this spawns a root shell because python3 runs as root via SUID. Type the full command.

wrong_answer_hints.response: sudo requires knowing the current user's password and having sudo rights configured. This exploit uses the SUID bit on python3 directly — no sudo needed.
su requires root's password. The SUID exploit bypasses authentication entirely by running python3 as root through the file permission bit.
python3 has no --shell flag. The exploit uses the -c flag to run inline Python code that calls os.execl to spawn a shell.

debrief.summary:
You identified python3.10 with the SUID bit misconfigured on
NexusCorp's server and escalated privileges to root. Mission complete.
From Caesar cipher to root shell — recon, enumeration, credential
attacks, web application exploitation, and privilege escalation.
The NexusCorp infiltration is finished.
debrief.real_world:
Privilege escalation findings are critical severity in every real
pentest report. SUID misconfigurations are common on systems where
admins install interpreters (Python, Perl, Ruby) without checking
the permission implications. GTFOBins is the go-to reference for
exploiting these in real engagements.
debrief.next_step:
Practice with real tools:
TryHackMe: "Linux Privilege Escalation" room
https://tryhackme.com/room/linprivesc
